points: Globalisation is rapid integration of countries through MNCs' foreign investments and trade, fragmenting production globally across multiple countries. MNCs design in developed countries, manufacture in low-cost regions, assemble elsewhere, and sell globally. Technology (containerization, telecommunications, internet) and liberalisation (removing trade barriers like tariffs and quotas) enable this integration. India's 1991 liberalisation opened the economy to globalisation, allowing MNCs to operate freely and local companies to compete globally.

Globalisation creates both winners and losers. Beneficiaries: consumers (more choice, lower prices), wealthy sections, educated workers in IT services earning high wages, large Indian companies becoming multinationals, urban areas. Losers: small producers unable to compete with large MNCs (like Ravi's capacitor unit), workers in declining sectors facing job losses and wage pressure, rural populations, vulnerable groups lacking skills. Example: Chinese toys displaced Indian toy makers; garment workers in export industries earn low wages with poor conditions; small Indian farmers struggle with imported cheap agricultural products. Benefits concentrate among the educated and wealthy while harms fall on poor and vulnerable.

WTO rules favor developed countries—they retain agricultural subsidies while forcing developing countries to remove support, creating unfair competition. For inclusive development, governments must intervene: protect workers through labour laws and minimum wages, support small producers with credit and technology, negotiate fairly in international organizations, expand education and infrastructure, and ensure basic services reach all. Without these interventions, globalisation reinforces existing inequalities rather than enabling development for all sections of society. Fair globalisation requires balancing global integration with domestic development and social protection.
